topic: How production CAD/CAE meshers (Rhino, gmsh/BAMG, CGAL, Netgen, OpenCASCADE BRepMesh, industry remeshers) achieve feature-preserving, anisotropy-aware surface meshing of parametric/displacement surfaces â€” and which techniques transfer to a registry-based watertight quadtree mesher
summary: Three findings answer the key questions. (1) FEATURE CURVES: every production mesher is curve-FIRST. The 1D feature/boundary curve is discretized once, to its own chord/angle tolerance, then FROZEN; the 2D face mesh is then forced to conform to that fixed polyline. Conformance is achieved four ways across the industry: (a) advancing-front growth outward FROM the curve so near-curve elements have edges along it, never spanning across it (Netgen edge-mesh-then-surface; OpenCASCADE BRepMesh edges-then-faces; Rhino meshes trim edges and connects the grid to them); (b) structured anisotropic RIBBONS extruded from the curve â€” gmsh's BoundaryLayer field lays N quad layers with geometric growth hwall*ratio^(dist/hwall) plus fans at corners, LayTracks lays rails from boundary to medial axis â€” giving exactly fine-perpendicular/coarse-along elements; (c) background-METRIC alignment (BAMG/gmsh): a metric tensor with its long eigenvector along the curve tangent makes the mesher stretch elements along a diagonal feature even on an axis-aligned domain; (d) cross-field-guided quasi-structured quads (gmsh's 2021 pipeline). Nobody CDT-fills axis-aligned cells against a diagonal constraint and accepts the raw angles. (2) QUALITY UNDER ANISOTROPY: no production anisotropic mesher bounds EUCLIDEAN parameter-space angles â€” they bound quality IN THE METRIC (unit-mesh principle: every edge ~unit length, every triangle ~equilateral measured in M). Labelleâ€“Shewchuk prove >=20 degrees measured in the metric of any point of the triangle; BAMG additionally bounds metric GRADATION (size-variation speed by log(ratio)) which is the standard guard against needles where anisotropy changes fast. Since the relevant metric on a parametric surface is the first fundamental form [E F; F G], in-metric angle = true 3D angle â€” exactly what crestBandTriangleQuality measures, and exactly what the dead earClipMaxMinAngle/efg path optimizes. When an extra mapping (helix warp) sits between the parameter domain and 3D, the standard practice is metric PULLBACK through the full composed map: M_domain = J_total^T J_total. (3) RHINO QUALITY for a displaced revolve concretely means: per-face structured quad grid in UV (aspect-ratio-bounded, >=16 initial quads, trims ignored), then recursive quad-split refinement until (default) the angle between surface normals across each polygon edge is <20 degrees AND edge-midpoint-to-surface distance is under tolerance, with min-edge cutoff; trim/edge polylines meshed once and shared so joined faces stitch watertight (Jagged seams off). Rhino guarantees NO minimum angle. Its actual displacement feature (ApplyDisplacement) is mesh-based: subdivide, contrast-driven edge splits, then 'Fairing' (smoothing rough feature edges) and a post-weld angle â€” strictly lower-fidelity than PotFoundry's analytic GPU evaluation. So matching 'Rhino quality' = the normal-divergence (max-angle) criterion + chordal sag, which the conforming mesher already meets or exceeds; beating Rhino on slivers requires the in-metric machinery Rhino itself lacks. Sources: Rhino detailed mesh options http://docs.mcneel.com/rhino/5/help/en-us/popup_moreinformation/polygon_mesh_detailed_options.htm and https://wiki.mcneel.com/rhino/meshfaq and https://docs.mcneel.com/rhino/8/help/en-us/properties/displacement.htm ; OCC https://dev.opencascade.org/doc/overview/html/occt_user_guides__mesh.html ; Netgen https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s007910050004 ; gmsh manual https://gmsh.info/doc/texinfo/gmsh.html ; BAMG https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228965458_bamg_Bidimensional_anisotropic_mesh_generator ; CGAL Mesh_3 https://doc.cgal.org/latest/Mesh_3/index.html ; Labelleâ€“Shewchuk https://www.ljll.fr/~frey/papers/meshing/Labelle%20F.,%20Anisotropic%20Voronoi%20diagrams%20and%20guaranteed-quality%20anisotropic%20mesh%20generation.pdf ; Bernâ€“Eppsteinâ€“Gilbert https://ics.uci.edu/~eppstein/pubs/BerEppGil-JCSS-94.pdf ; gmsh quasi-structured quads https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.04652 ; LayTracks https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/nme.1063 ; Alliez et al. https://geometry.caltech.edu/pubs/ACDLD03.pdf ; pullback-metric pipeline https://arxiv.org/pdf/2001.02542 ; Shimada bubble packing https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jrs/meshpapers/ShimadaYamadaItoh.pdf

techniques.name: In-metric (Riemannian) quality measurement and connectivity selection â€” the 'unit mesh' principle
techniques.source: BAMG (Hecht): https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228965458_bamg_Bidimensional_anisotropic_mesh_generator ; metric adaptation surveys: https://www.academia.edu/18139303/Anisotropic_metrics_for_mesh_adaptation ; gmsh manual (Algorithm=BAMG): https://gmsh.info/doc/texinfo/gmsh.html
techniques.howItWorks: All production anisotropic meshers define quality in a metric tensor field M, not in Euclidean parameter coordinates: the ideal mesh is 'unit' â€” every edge has length ~1 and every triangle is ~equilateral when measured in M. Edge swaps, diagonal choices, and point placement are chosen to maximize in-metric quality. For a parametric surface, M is the first fundamental form [E F; F G], so in-metric angles ARE the true 3D surface angles. BAMG performs swap/insertion decisions against this metric; gmsh exposes it via a background metric field.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Perfectly compatible: it is connectivity-only (vertex positions on shared cell edges untouched, registry intact, periodic u irrelevant). This is exactly the dead earClipMaxMinAngle/efg path in QuadtreeTriangulator.ts:100-191 â€” populating leaf.efg from firstFundamentalForm and letting the ear/diagonal selection maximize the min in-metric (=3D) angle is the industry-standard mechanism, validated by every metric-based mesher. Interior-only diagonal selection cannot break watertightness.

techniques.name: Pullback metric composition through extra mappings (J^T M J)
techniques.source: gmsh reparametrization pipeline: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2001.02542 ; anisotropic surface meshing literature: https://personal.utdallas.edu/~xguo/GMP2014.pdf , https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2461912.2461946
techniques.howItWorks: When the map from meshing domain to 3D is a composition (parametrization plus a warp/deformation), the metric used in the domain is pulled back through the FULL composed map: M_domain = J_total^T J_total where J_total is the Jacobian of the entire chain. Standard in every metric-based mesher; gmsh's discrete-model pipeline computes M(u,v)=J^T J/h^2 from the parametrization Jacobian. The mesher then squares/aligns elements in the domain so they come out well-shaped in 3D, including under shear.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Directly solves SpiralRidges: the helix shear (applyHelixWarp, ParametricExportComputer.ts:2466-2481) has an analytic constant Jacobian per (u,t); composing it into leaf.efg (warp-composed efg, handoff Step B) makes the existing in-metric diagonal selection see the parallelogram skew. No vertex positions change; registry and periodicity untouched.

techniques.name: Curve-first feature discretization, frozen, with the surrounding mesh conforming to it (edges-before-faces ordering)
techniques.source: OpenCASCADE BRepMesh: https://dev.opencascade.org/doc/overview/html/occt_user_guides__mesh.html ; Netgen advancing front: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s007910050004 ; Rhino two-stage meshing: https://wiki.mcneel.com/rhino/meshfaq
techniques.howItWorks: The universal CAD ordering: discretize all 1D entities (BRep edges, feature polylines) FIRST against their own chord/angle tolerance; that polyline is immutable; each adjacent face's interior mesh is then built to conform to it. Because both faces share the identical edge polyline, the result is watertight by construction with no stitching. Near the curve, elements are generated FROM the curve (advancing front outward or constrained insertion), so their edges lie along the feature rather than spanning across it.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Architecturally this is what the conforming mesher already does (FeatureConformingTriangulator inserts feature curves as constrained edges; the registry plays the role of the shared edge polyline). The gap vs industry practice is the FILL DIRECTION: production meshers grow elements from the curve outward, while the current code CDT-fills axis-aligned cells against the diagonal constraint, which manufactures cross-crest needles. Conclusion: keep the curve-first skeleton, change the near-curve fill (see ribbon and snap techniques).

techniques.name: Structured anisotropic ribbon / boundary-layer extrusion along feature curves
techniques.source: gmsh BoundaryLayer field (hwall*ratio^(dist/hwall), Quads, fans, AnisoMax): https://gmsh.info/doc/texinfo/gmsh.html and https://www.manpagez.com/info/gmsh/gmsh-2.8.4/gmsh_45.php ; LayTracks (Quadros): https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/nme.1063
techniques.howItWorks: Given a discretized curve, extrude N structured layers of quad (or split-quad) elements perpendicular to it with geometric growth â€” first layer height hwall, expansion ratio, total thickness â€” adding fans at sharp corners/endpoints; the ribbon's outer boundary becomes the new constraint that the isotropic background mesh conforms to. Result: crest-aligned anisotropic elements that are fine perpendicular to the curve and coarse along it â€” precisely the element shape a diagonal/helical crest needs to avoid sawtooth silhouettes. LayTracks generalizes this to whole domains via medial-axis 'tracks'.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Compatible IF the ribbon is generated as a deterministic, curve-local function (ribbon vertices derived only from the frozen crest polyline + analytic offsets in the metric) and its outer boundary is inserted through the same registry/CDT discipline â€” no repair needed, periodic u fine since the crest polyline is already periodic. It replaces the worst CDT cells (those the crest crosses) with structured elements. This is the heavyweight, industry-proven fix if in-metric diagonal selection alone leaves residual crest slivers; cost is real: ribbon/quadtree transition cells need careful constraint plumbing.

techniques.name: Vertex snapping / clearance around inserted features (Bernâ€“Eppsteinâ€“Gilbert warped quadtree; CGAL protecting balls)
techniques.source: BEG 'Provably Good Mesh Generation' (26.57â€“90 degree bounds on a balanced quadtree): https://ics.uci.edu/~eppstein/pubs/BerEppGil-JCSS-94.pdf ; CGAL Mesh_3 protecting balls: https://doc.cgal.org/latest/Mesh_3/index.html
techniques.howItWorks: Two complementary guards against feature-adjacent slivers. BEG: on a balanced quadtree, WARP (snap) grid vertices that fall within a tolerance of the feature onto the feature instead of keeping two nearly-coincident points, then triangulate crossing cells with templates â€” this is what makes their provable angle bounds survive feature insertion. CGAL: surround the feature polyline with protecting balls sized to local feature size; refinement may never insert a vertex inside a ball, guaranteeing the curve's segments survive as mesh edges and that no Steiner point lands pathologically close to the curve, with guaranteed termination at any dihedral angle.
techniques.fitToConstraints: The needle/sliver and the SFB@B=3 non-manifold signature (duplicated edges from dedupSide/weld at high anisotropy, FeatureConformingTriangulator.ts:713-734) are classic too-close-vertex-pair pathologies these rules exist to prevent. Adaptation: enforce an in-metric clearance when sampling the crest polyline per cell â€” if a crest sample falls within epsilon (measured in efg) of a registry/grid vertex, snap the CREST sample to that vertex (curve-local + registry-queryable, hence deterministic on both sides of a shared edge) instead of inserting near-duplicates; never move registry vertices themselves. Kills slivers at the source with no post-repair.

techniques.name: Metric gradation control (bounded anisotropy variation)
techniques.source: BAMG ratio/smoothing option (variation speed bounded by log(ratio)): https://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/focal/man1/bamg.1rheolef.html ; Alauzet metric adaptation: https://www.academia.edu/18139303/Anisotropic_metrics_for_mesh_adaptation
techniques.howItWorks: Production anisotropic meshers smooth the metric field before meshing so the size/stretch prescribed at neighboring points cannot jump faster than a gradation factor; BAMG bounds variation speed by log(ratio). Without it, abrupt anisotropy transitions (e.g., uBias B jumping between cells, or relief-gated GATE B switching on) generate exactly the needle elements seen in the 9/20 sliver regression.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Directly applicable to computeUBias: the GATE B re-baseline introduced slivers on 9 styles, which is the textbook symptom of un-graded metric jumps. Smoothing/graduating the per-region uBias (and any per-cell efg-derived sizing) across the quadtree before triangulation is cheap, position-free (affects only cell sizing decisions), and registry-safe.

techniques.name: Rhino's quality criteria: normal-divergence (max angle) refinement + chordal sag + aspect-bounded structured initial grid
techniques.source: Rhino detailed mesh options: http://docs.mcneel.com/rhino/5/help/en-us/popup_moreinformation/polygon_mesh_detailed_options.htm ; mesh FAQ: https://wiki.mcneel.com/rhino/meshfaq ; displacement properties: https://docs.mcneel.com/rhino/8/help/en-us/properties/displacement.htm
techniques.howItWorks: Rhino meshes each NURBS face as a structured UV quad grid (Maximum aspect ratio bounds initial quad shape; Minimum initial grid quads >=16, trims ignored), then recursively splits quads into 4 until: angle between surface normals across a polygon edge < Maximum angle (default 20 degrees), edge-midpoint-to-surface distance < Maximum distance edge-to-surface, edges within min/max edge length. Trim/edge polylines are meshed once and the grid is connected to them; 'Jagged seams' off = joined faces share edge discretizations = watertight. For displacement, Rhino does NOT evaluate analytically: ApplyDisplacement subdivides the render mesh, splits edges by texture contrast (Refine sensitivity), then applies 'Fairing' smoothing and a Post-weld angle. Rhino guarantees no minimum triangle angle anywhere.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Two takeaways. (a) 'Rhino quality' for a displaced revolve = the 20-degree normal-divergence bound + chordal sag on a stitched structured grid â€” adding a normal-divergence criterion alongside maxSag in the quadtree refinement predicate is small and registry-safe, and it is THE criterion users perceive as 'Rhino-smooth'. (b) PotFoundry's analytic GPU evaluation already exceeds Rhino's mesh-based displacement pipeline in fidelity; the competitive gap is purely triangle shape near features, which Rhino sidesteps with fairing (a smoothing pass the no-post-repair constraint forbids and fidelity goals reject anyway).

techniques.name: Labelleâ€“Shewchuk anisotropic Voronoi refinement (guaranteed in-metric angle >= 20 degrees)
techniques.source: Paper: https://www.ljll.fr/~frey/papers/meshing/Labelle%20F.,%20Anisotropic%20Voronoi%20diagrams%20and%20guaranteed-quality%20anisotropic%20mesh%20generation.pdf ; robust implementation (Schoen): https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jrs/papers/SchoenMS.pdf ; curved-boundary extension: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0010448508001656
techniques.howItWorks: The anisotropic analogue of Ruppert refinement: build an anisotropic Voronoi diagram (distance measured in each site's metric), refine by inserting sites until it dualizes to a triangulation in which no triangle has an angle below 20 degrees measured in the metric tensor of ANY point in the triangle. This is the strongest known quality guarantee under anisotropy and establishes the correct target: bound the IN-METRIC min angle (= 3D min angle on a surface), since Euclidean parameter-space angles can legitimately be arbitrarily sharp.
techniques.fitToConstraints: As an algorithm: incompatible (free point placement, no quadtree, would be a rebuild â€” and the project already proved Ruppert-style insertion unnecessary). As a SPECIFICATION: adopt its quality target â€” gate the crest band on in-metric/3D min angle >= ~20 degrees, which crestBandTriangleQuality already measures. Use it to define done, not to mesh.

techniques.name: Cross-field-guided, feature-aligned quasi-structured quad meshing
techniques.source: gmsh pipeline: https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.04652 ; Alliez et al. curvature-line remeshing: https://geometry.caltech.edu/pubs/ACDLD03.pdf ; Shimada ellipsoidal bubble packing: https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jrs/meshpapers/ShimadaYamadaItoh.pdf
techniques.howItWorks: Modern industry remeshers compute a smooth cross/direction field aligned to feature curves and principal curvature directions, then place points by frontal insertion guided by the field and a size map, producing quads whose edges follow features; gmsh's pipeline guarantees validity by monitoring element quality during every operation and reverting changes that violate it. Alliez et al. trace lines of curvature so elongated elements align with ridges by construction.
techniques.fitToConstraints: As a mesher: reject â€” it is a different architecture (global field computation, free placement) that would discard the quadtree/registry watertightness story; a rebuild with new failure modes. Two salvageable ideas: (a) the monitor-and-revert discipline â€” never accept a diagonal/template/insertion choice that drops local in-metric min angle below the gate, fall back to the safe choice (cheap to add to the efg ear-clip path); (b) the principle that element LONG axes must follow the feature tangent, which the ribbon technique delivers locally without a global field.

techniques.name: Sculpt-style overlay-grid feature capture with pillowing
techniques.source: Sandia Sculpt technical description: https://www.sandia.gov/files/cubit/15.5/help_manual/WebHelp/mesh_generation/meshing_schemes/parallel/sculpt_tech.htm ; octree sharp-feature hex meshing: https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-642-15414-0_15
techniques.howItWorks: Industry overlay-grid (octree) meshers capture sharp curves by extracting the feature graph first, snapping grid-adjacent nodes to it, then inserting a 'pillow' layer of elements along the captured curve to eliminate degenerate (triangle-shaped quad / doublet) elements that snapping creates. Sculpt itself documents that sharp features are the known weakness of overlay-grid methods unless explicitly captured.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Confirms the diagnosis from an independent industry lineage: axis-aligned grid + diagonal feature inherently produces degenerate elements unless you (a) snap nearby grid nodes to the curve and (b) insert a structured layer along it â€” i.e., the same snap+ribbon pair recommended above, here proven in production hex meshing. Pillowing per se is a post-pass on an existing mesh, so adopt the snap+layer ordering, not the literal post-hoc pillow.

keyInsight: Across every production lineage (Rhino/OCC/Netgen CAD meshers, BAMG/gmsh metric adaptation, CGAL, Sandia overlay grids), two invariants hold: features are meshed CURVE-FIRST and frozen with the surrounding mesh conforming to them, and triangle quality under anisotropy is bounded IN THE RIEMANNIAN METRIC (first fundamental form = true 3D angles), never in Euclidean parameter space. This means PotFoundry's dead efg/earClipMaxMinAngle path is not a speculative hack â€” it is precisely the industry-standard mechanism (BAMG-style in-metric connectivity selection), the helix warp is handled by the equally standard pullback-metric composition M = J_total^T J_total, and the residual crest-sliver tail has two proven, registry-compatible escalations: in-metric clearance/snapping of crest samples to nearby grid vertices (Bernâ€“Eppsteinâ€“Gilbert / CGAL protecting-ball discipline, kills near-duplicate-vertex slivers and likely the SFB non-manifold welds), and, if needed, a structured boundary-layer ribbon extruded from the crest polyline (gmsh BoundaryLayer / LayTracks) giving fine-perpendicular/coarse-along elements by construction. Notably, 'Rhino quality' is a LOWER bar than assumed: a 20-degree normal-divergence bound plus chordal sag on a stitched structured grid, with no min-angle guarantee and a mesh-based, faired displacement pipeline â€” the conforming mesher already exceeds it on fidelity; the remaining work is exactly the in-metric machinery Rhino itself does not have.

topic: High-quality triangulation under anisotropic metrics inside a fixed quadtree cell structure (cell-boundary vertices immutable): metric-aware diagonal/ear selection, anisotropic Delaunay refinement, surface-aware Steiner insertion, feature-aligned quadtree meshing, and by-construction sliver suppression
summary: Findings, keyed to the three questions. (Q1 â€” sheared metric, is diagonal choice enough?) No, and the limit is provable: with cell-boundary vertices immutable, a cell that the helix shear maps to a parallelogram with acute corner angle Î¸ admits NO triangulation â€” with or without interior Steiner points â€” whose min angle exceeds Î¸, because the corner angle is partitioned among incident triangles (one triangle gets â‰¤ Î¸ at that corner). For shear s (x'=x+sÂ·y), cosÎ¸ = s/âˆš(1+sÂ²): s=1â†’45Â°, s=2â†’26.6Â°, s=3.73â†’15Â°. So metric-aware diagonal/ear selection is sufficient ONLY while the sheared corner angle stays above target; beyond that, interior Steiner points along metric eigen-directions also cannot help â€” the literature fix is to align the LATTICE itself. D'Azevedo (SIAM J. Sci. Comput. 1991, https://epubs.siam.org/doi/10.1137/0912040) proved optimal meshes under a constant metric are regular lattices mapped through M^(-1/2) (i.e., shear the grid, not the diagonals); Loseille's metric-orthogonal/aligned point placement (https://www.researchgate.net/publication/267640909_Metric-orthogonal_Anisotropic_Mesh_Generation) is the modern practice: insert along metric eigenvectors, recovering locally structured anisotropic meshes. For SpiralRidges the warp is a known global affine-in-parameter shear applied post-triangulation, so building the quadtree in pre-sheared coordinates (u' = u âˆ’ sÂ·t) makes post-warp cells rectangular while preserving the registry (grid lines remain global, edge-local functions). (Q2 â€” ear-clip guarantees) Greedy ear-clipping by max-min angle has NO known optimality guarantee. Two exact results exist for a fixed polygon: (a) Klincsek's O(nÂ³) dynamic program computes the EXACT max-min-weight triangulation of a simple polygon for any decomposable weight â€” including min 3D angle from GPU-evaluated positions or warp-composed metric angle (Handbook of DCG ch.29, http://www.csun.edu/~ctoth/Handbook/chap29.pdf); trivially cheap at cell-polygon scale (nâ‰ˆ4â€“12) and interior-only â†’ registry-safe. (b) For a CONSTANT (frozen per-cell) metric, the metric is an affine image of Euclidean, so classical results transfer verbatim: Lawson flips with the metric circumcircle test terminate and yield the constrained Delaunay triangulation in that metric, which maximizes the min metric angle over all triangulations of the polygon/PSLG (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delaunay_triangulation). For spatially VARYING metrics the guarantee evaporates â€” the anisotropic Delaunay dual may not even be a triangulation (Labelleâ€“Shewchuk wedge/visibility conditions, https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/777792.777822; Boissonnat et al. revisit, https://inria.hal.science/inria-00336798v1/document), and the Bossenâ€“Heckbert averaged-tensor flip test is an unguaranteed heuristic (https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jrs/meshpapers/BossenHeckbert.pdf). Practice: freeze the metric per cell (cell-center efg, warp-composed) â†’ exact in-cell guarantees, deterministic across cells. This validates the dead earClipMaxMinAngle path's premise but says: replace greedy ear order with Klincsek DP (or frozen-metric Lawson flips) to get the certified polygon optimum at negligible cost. (Q3 â€” curved-surface insertion) The measured failure (flat-3D-plane off-center + barycentric back-map manufactures slivers) is the known pitfall: the back-map is exact only for affine charts. NO serious surface mesher inserts in the flat triangle plane. The parametric school (Borouchakiâ€“Laugâ€“George/BLSURF, https://recherche.utt.fr/medias/fichier/article-borouchaki-blsurf_1624803476614-pdf?ID_FICHE=26756&INLINE=FALSE) chooses the insertion LOCATION in the (u,v) parameter domain with all lengths/angles measured via the first-fundamental-form metric, then the 3D position is the EXACT surface evaluation of that (u,v) â€” precisely compatible with the registry + GPU evaluation. The restricted-Delaunay school (Chew; Boissonnatâ€“Oudot; CGAL Surface_mesher, https://doc.cgal.org/latest/Surface_mesher/index.html) inserts the intersection of the dual Voronoi edge with the surface itself; Engwirdaâ€“Ivers off-centres on curved surfaces (https://arxiv.org/abs/1501.04002) place off-centres on restricted-Voronoi faces with bounded radius-edge + convergence guarantees. ÃœngÃ¶r's off-centers (https://epubs.siam.org/doi/10.1137/080716748) remain valuable for WHERE along the Voronoi structure to aim (â‰ˆ40% fewer points), but the candidate must be expressed in parameter space and evaluated analytically, with a Bossenâ€“Heckbert-style accept/reject on the true 3D min angle. Also relevant context: Shewchuk's 'What Is a Good Linear Element?' (https://hal.science/hal-04614934/document) shows properly ALIGNED thin elements are harmless for accuracy â€” the printing/robustness goal of bounded 3D min angle is stricter than interpolation theory requires, so the corner-angle ceiling should be removed by alignment, not fought with refinement.

techniques.name: Klincsek dynamic-programming max-min-angle polygon triangulation (per-cell exact optimum)
techniques.source: Klincsek 1980; surveyed in Handbook of Discrete & Computational Geometry ch. 29 (Bern/Toth), http://www.csun.edu/~ctoth/Handbook/chap29.pdf
techniques.howItWorks: Number the polygon vertices around the boundary; DP over diagonals (i,j) choosing apex k to optimize any decomposable objective â€” here, maximize the minimum per-triangle weight where the weight is the TRUE 3D min angle computed from registry/GPU-evaluated vertex positions (or warp-composed metric angle). O(n^3) time, O(n^2) space, exact global optimum over all triangulations of the fixed simple polygon; only valid (in-polygon, in parameter space) diagonals considered.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Perfect fit: chooses only diagonals among existing vertices â€” no new points, boundary untouched, deterministic, interior-independent â†’ registry-safe and watertight by construction. At cell-polygon scale (nâ‰ˆ4â€“12 after feature-edge splits) O(n^3) is negligible. Directly upgrades the dead earClipMaxMinAngle path in QuadtreeTriangulator.ts:100-191 from unguaranteed greedy to certified optimum, and works for CDT-constrained cell sub-polygons too (triangulate each region between crest and cell boundary as its own simple polygon).

techniques.name: Frozen-per-cell-metric Lawson flips / metric constrained Delaunay (constant-metric pullback)
techniques.source: Lawson LOP + Delaunay max-min property (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delaunay_triangulation); anisotropic circumcircle flip test from Bossenâ€“Heckbert (https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jrs/meshpapers/BossenHeckbert.pdf); varying-metric failure modes in Labelleâ€“Shewchuk (https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/777792.777822) and Boissonnat et al. revisit (https://inria.hal.science/inria-00336798v1/document)
techniques.howItWorks: Evaluate ONE SPD metric tensor per cell (efg at cell center via firstFundamentalForm, composed with the helix-warp Jacobian for SpiralRidges), pull the cell back through M^(1/2) to Euclidean coordinates, and run ordinary Lawson flips / CDT there. Because the metric is constant within the cell, every classical guarantee transfers exactly: flips terminate, and the result maximizes the min METRIC angle among all triangulations of the cell polygon. With spatially varying tensors (per-vertex averaging Ã  la Bossenâ€“Heckbert) flips lose all guarantees and the anisotropic Delaunay dual may not be a triangulation â€” freeze per cell to stay in guaranteed territory.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Registry-safe (diagonal selection only). This is Step A of the existing handoff (populate leaf.efg) with a precise theoretical footing and one sharpening: do flips/CDT in the M^(1/2)-stretched coordinates rather than ad-hoc angle tests, and freeze the tensor per cell for determinism. Equivalent optimum to Klincsek DP for unconstrained cells; cheaper for large cells; Klincsek with true-3D-angle weight is strictly more faithful to the measured sliver metric. Also likely fixes the SFB CDT robustness issue: running cdt2d + dedupSide in metric-stretched (well-separated) coordinates instead of raw biased coordinates removes the near-coincident-point welding that produces the B=3 non-manifold edges.

techniques.name: Shear-aligned lattice via coordinate transformation (d'Azevedo) â€” build the quadtree in warp-composed coordinates for SpiralRidges
techniques.source: D'Azevedo, 'Optimal Triangular Mesh Generation by Coordinate Transformation', SIAM J. Sci. Comput. 1991, https://epubs.siam.org/doi/10.1137/0912040; exposition: https://www.cs.harvard.edu/~sjg/papers/optMesh.pdf; directional Cartesian refinement precedent: https://www.nas.nasa.gov/publications/software/docs/cart3d/pages/publications/aftosmis_97vkiNotes.pdf
techniques.howItWorks: Under a constant metric M, the optimal mesh is a regular lattice mapped through M^(-1/2): equilateral-in-metric elements come from transforming the GRID, not from choosing diagonals of a misaligned grid. For a pure shear (the helix warp u'=u+sÂ·t), generate the quadtree over sheared parameter coordinates so that the post-warp cells are rectangles; the shear is a single global affine reparametrization, so grid lines remain globally defined curves and any vertex on a shared cell edge is still an edge-local function â€” the registry guarantee survives intact.
techniques.fitToConstraints: This is the ONLY fix for the provable corner-angle ceiling: once the effective shear makes cell corner angles < target (cosÎ¸ = s/âˆš(1+sÂ²)), no in-cell triangulation â€” diagonals OR interior Steiner â€” can avoid sub-target angles, because boundary vertices are immutable and the corner angle is partitioned among incident triangles. chooseHelixGrid/applyHelixWarp (ParametricExportComputer.ts:2466-2481) already knows the shear analytically; moving it from post-triangulation vertex warp to pre-triangulation domain shear is a contained change to one style's grid construction. This is the handoff's Step B (warp-composed efg) taken to its logical conclusion â€” compose the warp into the DOMAIN, not just the metric.

techniques.name: Parameter-space Steiner insertion under the first-fundamental-form metric (surface-exact positions, never flat-plane back-mapping)
techniques.source: Borouchaki, Laug, George â€” BLSURF, 'Parametric surface meshing using a combined advancing-front generalized-Delaunay approach', https://recherche.utt.fr/medias/fichier/article-borouchaki-blsurf_1624803476614-pdf?ID_FICHE=26756&INLINE=FALSE; Laugâ€“Borouchaki 'Interpolating and meshing 3D surface grids', https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/nme.770
techniques.howItWorks: All point-placement decisions (sizes, angles, off-center positions) are made in the (u,t) parameter domain with distances measured by the induced Riemannian metric (first fundamental form E,F,G â€” warp-composed where applicable); the 3D vertex is then the EXACT analytic surface evaluation of the chosen (u,t). The flat 3D triangle plane is never used to manufacture coordinates, so there is no affine back-map error â€” the step that was measured to manufacture slivers in refineCellInterior simply does not exist in this formulation.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Directly answers Q3 and matches the existing architecture: the conforming mesher already GPU-evaluates true surface positions from (u,t), so the only change is that interior Steiner candidates are computed as (u,t) coordinates optimized in the metric (e.g., metric off-center / metric incenter of the bad triangle's parameter-space preimage), not as 3D plane points barycentrically back-mapped. Interior insertions never touch cell-edge vertices â†’ registry-safe. Pair with an accept/reject check on the measured 3D min angle (Bossenâ€“Heckbert style) so any residual metric-vs-3D mismatch can only improve the mesh.

techniques.name: Metric-orthogonal / metric-aligned interior point placement (Loseille) â€” Steiner points along metric eigenvectors
techniques.source: Loseille, 'Metric-orthogonal Anisotropic Mesh Generation' (IMR 2014), https://www.researchgate.net/publication/267640909_Metric-orthogonal_Anisotropic_Mesh_Generation; follow-ups: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0010448521001470
techniques.howItWorks: Frontal insertion of new points along the eigenvector directions of the local metric, combined with a cavity operator, producing locally structured anisotropic meshes whose elements are aligned with the metric (angle distributions concentrate at the needed small angles and at right angles, suppressing arbitrary slivers). The crest case maps directly: eigen-directions = crest tangent (coarse) and crest normal (fine), giving the fine-perpendicular/coarse-along elements the diagonal-crest serration needs.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Adaptable as the WAY to choose interior (u,t) Steiner locations inside a cell next to an inserted crest edge: walk outward from the crest along the metric-normal eigenvector at metric-unit spacing, insert as constrained-CDT interior points, evaluate on the true surface. Interior-only â†’ registry-safe. Caveats: (1) it cannot beat the cell corner-angle ceiling (see d'Azevedo entry) â€” use after/with grid alignment; (2) full cavity-based reinsertion as in Loseille's remesher moves existing vertices and is NOT registry-safe â€” only the point-placement rule transfers, not the cavity operator.

techniques.name: Offset-curve crest ribbons: boundary-layer structure expressed as additional GLOBAL constraint curves
techniques.source: Advancing-layer / boundary-layer anisotropic meshing: 'Anisotropic sources for surface and volume boundary layer mesh generation', https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S002199912030629X; closed advancing-layer: https://pages.saclay.inria.fr/frederic.alauzet/proceedings/Feuillet_A%20closed%20advancing-layer%20method%20for%20generating%20curved%20boundary%20layer%20mesh.pdf; semi-structured layers around feature curves: https://blogs.sw.siemens.com/simcenter/why-anisotropic-meshing-increases-your-happiness/
techniques.howItWorks: CFD boundary-layer meshers march structured anisotropic layers perpendicular to a wall/feature curve, giving by-construction high-quality thin elements aligned with the feature. Translated to this pipeline: instead of per-cell layer marching (which would violate cell independence), compute 1â€“3 OFFSET curves of each crest feature curve in the parameter domain (crest Â± Î´_k along the metric normal) and feed them to the SAME FeatureConformingTriangulator machinery as extra constraint curves. The region between crest and first offset becomes a ribbon of well-shaped quad-like pairs; cell-edge crossings of an offset curve are functions of (global curve, grid line) only â€” exactly as edge-local as the existing crest insertions.
techniques.fitToConstraints: By-construction sliver suppression at the exact failure site (axis-aligned cells beside a diagonal crest CDT-filled with long thin triangles spanning the crest): the offset curve interposes a graded boundary so no triangle spans from the crest to a far cell corner. Reuses existing, registry-proven constraint-curve insertion; no new topology mechanism. Cost: more constrained edges per crest cell (perf), and offset curves of high-curvature crests need care (cusps/self-intersections of offsets â€” standard offset-trimming issue).

techniques.name: Grid-vertex warping/snapping onto feature curves (Bernâ€“Eppsteinâ€“Gilbert / Liangâ€“Zhang style) instead of inserting feature edges across cells
techniques.source: Bern, Eppstein, Gilbert, 'Provably Good Mesh Generation', JCSS 1994, https://ics.uci.edu/~eppstein/pubs/BerEppGil-JCSS-94.pdf; Liang, Ebeida, Zhang, 'Guaranteed-quality all-quadrilateral mesh generation with feature preservation', CMAME 2010, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0045782510000836 (angles in [45Â°,135Â°] except where input geometry forces worse)
techniques.howItWorks: The classical guaranteed-quality quadtree meshers do NOT CDT-insert feature segments across axis-aligned cells; they WARP the nearest grid corner onto the feature point/curve (replace corner y by feature point x) and retriangulate with templates, preserving provable angle bounds (30Â°â€“90Â° triangles in improved variants; [45Â°,135Â°] quads in Liangâ€“Zhang). Warping eliminates the thin wedge between a diagonal feature edge and a nearby parallel cell boundary by removing the near-duplicate vertex pair at its source.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Registry-compatible IF the snap rule is edge-local: a grid corner is a function of two global grid lines, and 'snap corner to crest curve if metric-distance < tolerance' depends only on (global curve, grid lines) â€” both neighbours derive the same snapped position bit-identically. This is a deeper change to FeatureConformingTriangulator (feature handling moves from in-cell CDT to grid deformation) but it is the only surveyed approach with PROVEN angle bounds in the presence of features inside a quadtree, and it attacks the serration mechanism (crest-adjacent thin wedges) by construction rather than by repair.

techniques.name: Labelleâ€“Shewchuk anisotropic Voronoi refinement (20Â°-in-metric guarantee)
techniques.source: Labelle & Shewchuk, 'Anisotropic Voronoi Diagrams and Guaranteed-Quality Anisotropic Mesh Generation', SoCG 2003, https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/777792.777822; implementability critique: Boissonnat et al., https://inria.hal.science/inria-00336798v1/document; robust variant: Schoen MS thesis, https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jrs/papers/SchoenMS.pdf
techniques.howItWorks: Defines a Voronoi diagram where distance to each site is measured in that site's own metric; refines until every Voronoi cell sees its site ('wedge' visibility), at which point the dual is a valid triangulation with no angle < 20Â° measured in the metric of any point in each triangle. The theory anchor for what 'guaranteed-quality anisotropic' even means.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Reject as an algorithm: it requires global freedom to place sites anywhere (incompatible with an immutable quadtree vertex registry), its bisectors are curved and hard to compute robustly (the Boissonnat revisit and Schoen thesis both center on this), and its guarantee is in-metric, not the 3D Euclidean min angle the export measures. Valuable as theory: it formalizes why varying-metric flips/duals fail (non-triangulation duals) and motivates the frozen-per-cell-metric simplification adopted above.

techniques.name: Duâ€“Wang ACVT / LÃ©vyâ€“Liu LpCVT variational anisotropic remeshing
techniques.source: Du & Wang, 'Anisotropic Centroidal Voronoi Tessellations and Their Applications', https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/1c8fee8fb03bdeb7363436ba5fb76b03170334e3; LÃ©vy & Liu, 'Lp Centroidal Voronoi Tessellation and its applications', ACM TOG 2010, https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1778765.1778856 (pdf: https://www.cs.jhu.edu/~misha/ReadingSeminar/Papers/Levy10.pdf)
techniques.howItWorks: Globally optimizes ALL vertex positions to minimize a (directionally weighted) CVT energy â€” Lloyd iterations under a directional distance (ACVT) or Newton on a closed-form Lp moment objective with a background tensor field (LpCVT) â€” yielding metric-adapted, well-shaped anisotropic elements and quad-dominant alignment.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Reject for in-cell use: these are global variational methods whose entire power comes from moving every vertex; with cell-boundary vertices immutable, the energy cannot be meaningfully minimized, and running them interior-only per tiny cell degenerates to a smoothing pass that the measured refineCellInterior result already discredits unless positions are re-derived in parameter space. The one transferable idea â€” optimize interior (u,t) positions against a metric objective and evaluate on the surface â€” is already covered by the BLSURF/Loseille entries at far lower machinery cost.

techniques.name: Restricted-Delaunay / on-surface off-centres (Chew, Boissonnatâ€“Oudot, Engwirdaâ€“Ivers) with accept/reject insertion
techniques.source: CGAL Surface_mesher (Boissonnatâ€“Oudot), https://doc.cgal.org/latest/Surface_mesher/index.html; Engwirda & Ivers, 'Off-centre Steiner points for Delaunay-refinement on curved surfaces', https://arxiv.org/abs/1501.04002 (journal: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0010448515001608); ÃœngÃ¶r off-centers, https://epubs.siam.org/doi/10.1137/080716748 and https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-540-24698-5_19; accept/reject discipline: Bossenâ€“Heckbert, https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jrs/meshpapers/BossenHeckbert.pdf
techniques.howItWorks: Refinement points are never the flat-plane circumcenter: Boissonnatâ€“Oudot insert the intersection of the dual Voronoi edge with the surface itself (always an on-surface point); Engwirdaâ€“Ivers place size-optimal off-centres on restricted-Voronoi faces, proving bounded radius-edge ratios and convergence while inserting ~40% fewer points than circumcenter refinement (ÃœngÃ¶r). Bossenâ€“Heckbert accept a local operation only if it measurably improves the objective.
techniques.fitToConstraints: The principle â€” refinement candidates live ON the surface (here: in parameter space, evaluated analytically), and 'where along the Voronoi structure' follows ÃœngÃ¶r's shortest-edge-anchored off-center rule transcribed into the frozen cell metric â€” is the corrected replacement for the ruled-out flat-plane refineCellInterior. The accept/reject wrapper (insert only if measured 3D min angle improves) makes any insertion scheme safe against metric/3D mismatch at trivial cost. Full restricted-Delaunay machinery (3D Voronoi of samples) is unnecessary given an analytic parametrization â€” adapt the placement rules only.

techniques.name: Dual contouring / dual marching squares with Hermite feature data (feature-aligned dual meshes on a fixed grid)
techniques.source: Ju, Losasso, Schaefer, Warren, 'Dual Contouring of Hermite Data', SIGGRAPH 2002, https://www.cs.rice.edu/~jwarren/papers/dualcontour.pdf (ACM: https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/566654.566586); Manifold Dual Contouring: https://people.engr.tamu.edu/schaefer/research/dualsimp_tvcg.pdf
techniques.howItWorks: Keeps the grid fixed but places ONE free vertex per cell minimizing a QEF built from exact feature intersections+normals on cell edges (Hermite data); sharp features are captured because the per-cell vertex lands on the feature automatically, and the mesh is crack-free across adaptive octree/quadtree levels. The dual formulation means no axis-aligned cell boundary ever cuts across a feature â€” feature alignment is intrinsic, not inserted.
techniques.fitToConstraints: A coherent alternative paradigm to the current primal registry mesher (it solves the diagonal-crest problem at the formulation level), and its edge-intersection data is exactly what the feature curves already provide. But it replaces the entire primal cell-triangulation + registry design rather than fitting inside it (vertices are per-CELL, shared via cell adjacency, watertightness reasoning is different), so it is a rebuild â€” flag for a future architecture discussion only; do not pursue for the current cutover.

keyInsight: The fixed cell boundary imposes a provable ceiling: a cell sheared (in 3D or in metric) to a parallelogram with acute corner angle Î¸ admits no triangulation with min angle > Î¸ â€” interior Steiner points and diagonal choice share this cap, so the helix-shear answer to Q1 is "diagonal choice suffices only while cosÎ¸ = s/âˆš(1+sÂ²) stays above the target; beyond that, align the lattice (d'Azevedo: build the quadtree in warp-composed/sheared coordinates â€” registry-safe because the shear is a global affine reparametrization), don't fight the corners." Within that ceiling, stop using greedy ear-clipping (no guarantee exists): per cell, freeze ONE metric tensor (warp-composed efg at cell center) â€” constant metric is an affine pullback of Euclidean, so Lawson flips/CDT regain exact termination and max-min-angle optimality â€” or, strictly better for the measured objective, run Klincsek's O(nÂ³) DP on each tiny cell polygon with the TRUE 3D min angle as weight to get the certified optimum interior-only and registry-safe. And for Q3, the literature unanimously confirms the measurement: never manufacture points in the flat triangle plane â€” choose Steiner locations in (u,t) under the first-fundamental-form metric (BLSURF; ÃœngÃ¶r/Engwirda off-center rules transcribed into the frozen metric), evaluate positions analytically on the true surface, and gate every insertion with an accept-only-if-3D-min-angle-improves check.

topic: Pipeline alternatives for exporting a smooth displaced revolve with sharp feature curves â€” vs fixing the existing periodic-quadtree conforming mesher
summary: Honest read after surveying the literature: the current architecture (2:1-balanced periodic quadtree in (u,t) + edge-local vertex registry + per-cell constrained CDT + GPU evaluation on the analytic surface) is already the textbook-correct combination â€” it is exactly the 'restricted quadtree + edge-invariant shared-edge vertices' discipline that the crack-free tessellation literature (Niessner feature-adaptive subdivision, hardware-tessellation crack rules, Nanite locked cluster boundaries, the 2025 WATER paper) prescribes for watertightness by construction, fused with classical CAD parametric-domain conforming meshing. None of the five alternatives beats it on the hard constraints. (a) OpenSubdiv adaptive subdivision with semi-sharp creases is crack-free and seam-free, but its limit surface is NOT the analytic displaced revolve â€” projecting limit vertices back to the true surface destroys the exactness chain near high curvature, and the hard part (a crest-aligned coarse cage) is the same unsolved alignment problem; it also drags a C++ library into a WASM build for a problem the global (u,t) parameterization already solves. (b) Dual contouring (even Manifold DC) fails three constraints at once: QEF vertices are off-surface by construction, basic DC is non-manifold / MDC can still self-intersect, and the pot's thin double wall forces octree cells finer than min wall thickness everywhere or the genus silently changes â€” a rebuild that lands strictly worse. (d) Instant-Meshes/QuadriFlow-style remeshing is an optimizer, not a construction: vertices are relaxed off the surface every iteration, watertightness is empirical not guaranteed (QuadriFlow exists precisely because Instant Meshes leaves holes/non-manifold spots), and the mandatory validate-and-fix step violates the NO-repair constraint outright. (e) GPU fractional tessellation contributes nothing new â€” its crack-avoidance invariant (edge tess factor a function of edge-only data) is precisely the registry the project already has, fractional modes deliberately MANUFACTURE sliver transition triangles (fine for temporal smoothness on screen, bad for printing), and tessellation patterns cannot honor a feature curve crossing a patch interior. The only alternative with real merit is (c) curve-first meshing â€” mesh the feature polylines under the surface metric first, then fill with metric-driven (Riemannian) advancing-front/Delaunay in the parameter domain (BLSURF / Tristano-Owen lineage). It is the principled fix for the diagnosed root cause (axis-aligned cells beside diagonal crests; uBias is a global axis-aligned approximation of a local, direction-aware metric), it keeps every vertex generated in (u,t) and thus exactly on the analytic surface, and the periodic seam yields to the same registry/index-weld trick. But it replaces the quadtree+registry topology guarantee with advancing-front robustness engineering â€” months of work the literature says is finicky â€” while the codebase already contains the 80% version as verified dead code: QuadtreeTriangulator.ts:100-191 has metric-aware (efg) max-min-angle ear-clip/diagonal selection, gated on leaf.efg which PeriodicBalancedQuadtree never populates, and firstFundamentalForm already exists and is exported. Sequence: activate efg (plain, then warp-composed for SpiralRidges), measure with crestBandTriangleQuality; only if the measured ceiling of diagonal-only reshaping is hit (it cannot move cell-boundary vertices, so a residual is plausible), escalate to feature-adjacent anisotropic fill (c-lite inside cells), and keep full curve-first global meshing as the documented fallback â€” not the next step.

techniques.name: (a) Adaptive subdivision surfaces (OpenSubdiv, semi-sharp creases) + projection to the analytic surface
techniques.source: Niessner et al., Feature-Adaptive GPU Rendering of Catmull-Clark Subdivision Surfaces â€” http://www.graphics.stanford.edu/~niessner/papers/2012/3feature/niessner2012feature.pdf ; OpenSubdiv subdivision docs (crease sharpness 0-10, isolation levels) â€” https://github.com/PixarAnimationStudios/OpenSubdiv/blob/release/documentation/subdivision_surfaces.rst ; Stam, Exact Evaluation of Catmull-Clark Surfaces (SIGGRAPH 98) â€” https://www.dgp.toronto.edu/public_user/stam/reality/Research/pdf/sig98.pdf ; Lee/Moreton/Hoppe, Displaced Subdivision Surfaces â€” https://www.researchgate.net/publication/2486725_Displaced_Subdivision_Surfaces
techniques.howItWorks: Build a coarse quad cage approximating the displaced revolve; tag feature curves as semi-sharp/infinitely-sharp creases; feature-adaptive subdivision isolates irregular features with crack-free transition patches; evaluate the limit surface (exactly, via Stam eigenbasis); optionally displace/project each limit vertex onto the true analytic surface as a final step.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Watertight-by-construction: YES â€” adaptive subdivision with restricted-quadtree transition patches is provably crack-free, and a closed cage has no seam at all (periodic u handled for free). Exactness: NO â€” the Catmull-Clark limit surface is not the analytic displaced revolve; the projection step restores on-surface vertices but is a post-hoc move that can fold/self-intersect where the projection is non-injective (dense ripples, sharp crests), reintroducing exactly the sliver/overlap class being fought. Sharp-feature exactness: only as good as the cage â€” crease edges must lie ON the analytic crest curves, which is the same crest-extraction-and-alignment problem already solved in FeatureConformingTriangulator, now re-solved in cage topology. Crucially, subdivision refines isotropically in cage parameterization, so the diagonal-crest anisotropy problem (the actual diagnosed defect) persists unless the cage is crest-aligned â€” the hard part again. Plus OpenSubdiv is C++ (WASM build) and cage generation for 20 procedural styles is a new pipeline.

techniques.name: (b) Dual contouring / Manifold DC on a 3D field with sharp-feature preservation
techniques.source: Schaefer/Ju/Warren, Manifold Dual Contouring â€” https://www.cs.wustl.edu/~taoju/research/dualsimp_tvcg.pdf ; Schaefer/Warren, Dual Contouring: The Secret Sauce â€” https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~jrs/meshpapers/SchaeferWarren2.pdf ; Rashid et al., Watertight 2-manifold DC via tetrahedral decomposition â€” https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1877705816333422 ; DC pitfalls overview â€” https://www.boristhebrave.com/2018/04/15/dual-contouring-tutorial/
techniques.howItWorks: Define an implicit inside/outside field for the pot (easy here: r - R(u,t) heightfield over the revolve), sample Hermite data (edge intersections + normals) on an adaptive octree, place one vertex per cell minimizing a QEF (which reproduces sharp creases/corners from normal discontinuities), emit quads dual to sign-changing edges. Manifold DC and tetrahedral-decomposition variants patch the manifoldness holes of basic DC.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Fails three hard constraints simultaneously. Exactness: NO, structurally â€” QEF minimizers live anywhere in the cell, not on the analytic surface; in ambiguous cells implementations fall back to the mass point to avoid spikes, drifting further. Watertight/manifold: basic DC is crack-free on octrees but NON-manifold when two sheets cross one cell â€” which is the normal case for this geometry: a pot is a thin double wall (outer + inner + rim), so any cell coarser than local wall thickness merges the sheets and silently changes genus or punches holes; Manifold DC restores manifoldness but still admits self-intersections. Sharp features: approximate (Hermite-data quality), corners can spike. Periodic seam: genuinely a non-issue (volumetric) â€” the one real advantage. Resolution economics are wrong for 'facet-free below printer resolution': octree must be globally fine near every feature AND finer than min wall thickness, exploding triangle counts vs the parametric mesher's surface-adapted budget. A full rebuild that lands strictly worse on the stated goals.

techniques.name: (c) Curve-first meshing: feature polylines first, then Riemannian-metric advancing-front/Delaunay fill in the (u,t) domain
techniques.source: Tristano/Owen/Canann, Advancing Front Surface Mesh Generation in Parametric Space Using a Riemannian Surface Definition â€” https://www.ljll.fr/~frey/papers/meshing/Tristano%20J.R.,%20Advancing%20front%20surface%20mesh%20generation%20in%20parametric%20space%20using%20a%20Riemannian%20surface%20definition.pdf ; Borouchaki/Laug, BLSURF-style combined advancing-front/Delaunay parametric meshing â€” https://recherche.utt.fr/medias/fichier/article-borouchaki-blsurf_1624803476614-pdf?ID_FICHE=26756&INLINE=FALSE ; CGAL 2D periodic triangulations (notes cylindrical periodicity; constrained periodic CDT not off-the-shelf) â€” https://doc.cgal.org/latest/Periodic_2_triangulation_2/index.html ; CGAL Delaunay_mesher_2 â€” https://doc.cgal.org/latest/Mesh_2/classCGAL_1_1Delaunay__mesher__2.html
techniques.howItWorks: Classical CAD-kernel surface meshing (what Rhino-class meshers actually do): discretize feature curves FIRST as polylines whose segment lengths satisfy sag bounds measured in the surface metric; then fill the regions between curves with advancing-front or Delaunay refinement performed in the parameter domain but with all lengths/angles measured under the first fundamental form (Riemannian metric), so triangles come out crest-aligned, graded, and near-equilateral in 3D regardless of parameterization shear â€” including helix shear, by composing the warp into the metric.
techniques.fitToConstraints: The only alternative that attacks the diagnosed root cause head-on: serration = axis-aligned cells beside diagonal crests, and a metric-driven fill produces fine-perpendicular/coarse-along elements at the crest BY construction; the global uBias gate is revealed as a crude axis-aligned approximation of this local metric. Exactness: YES â€” every vertex is generated in (u,t) and GPU-evaluated on the analytic surface, same as today; feature polyline vertices lie exactly on crest curves. Watertight: YES but by GLOBAL construction, not local â€” a single triangulation of the domain is trivially manifold; the periodic seam needs the same trick as today (cut at u=0, constrain identical t-samples both sides, weld by index â€” exact since the sampler is exactly periodic; CGAL's periodic package shows 1-direction periodicity is possible but constrained periodic CDT is not off-the-shelf). Costs: abandons the quadtree+registry+per-cell-CDT core (most of the ~12.6k-line conforming directory), and metric advancing-front is notoriously finicky to make robust (front collisions, metric discontinuities at crest junctions, the 'dirty geometry' literature exists for a reason) â€” months to reach the current 20/20-watertight baseline. Reusable today: firstFundamentalForm, feature-curve extraction, samplers, WatertightAssembly, all fidelity instruments (~30-50%). Verdict: the principled ENDGAME, not the next step â€” hold as documented fallback if metric-aware fixes inside the quadtree hit their ceiling.

techniques.name: (d) Isotropic/field-aligned remesh + feature-snap post-pass (Instant Meshes / QuadriFlow with feature locks)
techniques.source: Jakob et al., Instant Field-Aligned Meshes â€” https://www.researchgate.net/publication/283334005_Instant_Field-Aligned_Meshes ; QuadriFlow repo (-sharp flag; built to fix Instant Meshes' holes/non-manifold output via min-cost flow on singularities) â€” https://github.com/hjwdzh/QuadriFlow ; Hu et al., Error-Bounded and Feature-Preserving Surface Remeshing â€” https://arxiv.org/pdf/1611.02147
techniques.howItWorks: Take the already-exported mesh, compute orientation + position fields (optionally aligned/snapped to detected sharp edges), and locally relax vertices toward field-consistent positions to produce uniform, well-shaped quads/tris; feature locks constrain marked edges; optionally re-project vertices to the original surface after each relaxation round.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Structurally incompatible with the hard constraints â€” this is the canonical 'why post-remeshing conflicts with exact-on-surface vertices' case. Exactness: every relaxation step moves vertices OFF the analytic surface (local averaging in 3D/tangent planes); re-projection restores position but not the error-bound chain, and resampled feature polylines no longer lie exactly on the analytic crest curves unless snapped to a parametric curve oracle â€” extra machinery that still leaves the smooth regions trusting projection convergence near dense ripples. Watertight: NOT guaranteed â€” these are greedy/optimization methods; QuadriFlow's stated reason to exist is that Instant Meshes leaves holes and non-manifold vertices, and QuadriFlow's own -sharp feature detection is acknowledged as unreliable; shipping this means a mandatory validate-and-repair pass, which the project constraints forbid outright (and which the legacy 1100-line repair battery already demonstrated is a tar pit). Periodic seam: moot (input closed). Triangle QUALITY would genuinely improve â€” but at the cost of every guarantee the rebuild was undertaken to obtain.

techniques.name: (e) GPU tessellation-shader-style adaptive tessellation with crack-free transitions (fractional modes, Nanite-style boundary locking) adapted to export
techniques.source: Nathan Reed, Tessellation Modes Quick Reference (edge-factor symmetry/crack rules, fractional partitioning) â€” https://www.reedbeta.com/blog/tess-quick-ref/ ; Niessner, Rendering Subdivision Surfaces using Hardware Tessellation (thesis; restricted-quadtree crack avoidance) â€” http://www.graphics.stanford.edu/~niessner/papers/2013/1thesis/niessner2013thesis.pdf ; Karis/Stubbe/Wihlidal, Nanite â€” A Deep Dive (SIGGRAPH 2021; cluster boundary edges locked across LODs) â€” https://advances.realtimerendering.com/s2021/Karis_Nanite_SIGGRAPH_Advances_2021_final.pdf ; WATER: Watertight Tessellation for Pixel-Accurate Rendering (2025) â€” https://www.researchgate.net/publication/398335537_WATER_Watertight_Tessellation_for_Real-Time_Pixel-Accurate_Rendering_of_Large-Scale_Surfaces
techniques.howItWorks: Per-patch tessellation factors driven by a screen/sag error metric; cracks between neighboring patches avoided by computing each shared edge's factor from edge-only data (so both sides agree bit-identically) plus symmetric vertex placement under endpoint swap; fractional modes blend non-integer factors smoothly; Nanite avoids LOD cracks by locking cluster-boundary edges within a group; domain shader evaluates the true surface at generated parameter coordinates.
techniques.fitToConstraints: This is the one to 'adopt' â€” except it already HAS been: the crack-free invariant of the entire genre ('shared-edge vertices must be an edge-local function so both neighbors derive identical points') is verbatim the grid-line vertex registry, and 2:1 quadtree balance is the restricted-quadtree transition rule from the same literature; the conforming mesher IS the export-grade instantiation of this technique. What's left to import is actively harmful or irrelevant for export: fractional partitioning exists for temporally smooth LOD on screen and deliberately generates pairs of tiny transition slivers near edge endpoints/midpoints â€” the precise defect class being eliminated for printing (integer factors + balanced transitions, i.e. the status quo, are correct); tessellation patterns are oblivious to feature curves crossing a patch interior, so sharp features must become patch boundaries â€” which collapses back into the curve-first cage-layout problem of (a)/(c); periodic u is handled today via the registry, and tessellation hardware semantics add nothing there. Exactness is equivalent (evaluate analytic surface at generated (u,t)). No new pipeline value.

techniques.name: (f) Baseline comparator â€” metric-driven triangulation INSIDE the existing quadtree: populate leaf.efg, activate the dead max-min-angle ear-clip, warp-compose for SpiralRidges, then feature-adjacent anisotropic fill
techniques.source: Verified in-repo: QuadtreeTriangulator.ts:100-191 (shapedTemplate, earClipMaxMinAngle, triMinAngle3D under efg metric), PeriodicBalancedQuadtree.ts:65 (efg? declared, never assigned â€” grep-confirmed dead), firstFundamentalForm exported from SurfaceMetricTensor; literature validation that metric-tensor-driven element shaping is the standard cure for direction-misaligned slivers: Mmg/ParMmg metric-based adaptation â€” https://arxiv.org/pdf/2201.02806 ; Loseille, Metric-Orthogonal Anisotropic Mesh Generation â€” https://www.researchgate.net/publication/267640909_Metric-orthogonal_Anisotropic_Mesh_Generation
techniques.howItWorks: Step A: in PeriodicBalancedQuadtree.leafOfCell, set leaf.efg = firstFundamentalForm(metric, uc, tc, hu, ht) â€” the already-written shapedTemplate gate then routes anisotropic cells through earClipMaxMinAngle, which picks diagonals/ears maximizing the min 3D angle under the metric while leaving polygon-boundary vertices untouched (registry/watertightness preserved by construction). Step B: for SpiralRidges, compose the helix shear Jacobian into efg so the metric sees post-warp 3D lengths. Step C: cap/repair-free the CDT auto-B non-manifold (FeatureConformingTriangulator dedup at high anisotropy) and add metric-aware fill in cells adjacent to inserted feature edges.
techniques.fitToConstraints: Perfect by definition: zero change to watertightness machinery (interior-only diagonal selection), vertices stay exactly on the analytic surface, periodic seam untouched, byte-identical output on cells the aniso gate doesn't fire on (re-baseline only where it does). The anisotropic-adaptation literature confirms this is the right lever â€” slivers from direction-misaligned elements are cured by measuring under the metric tensor, exactly what efg enables. Honest ceiling to verify by measurement: diagonal/ear re-selection cannot MOVE cell-boundary vertices, so a sliver whose bad angle is pinned at the cell boundary geometry is untouchable from inside â€” if crestBandTriangleQuality shows a residual after Steps A+B, that is the trigger to escalate toward (c)'s metric-driven fill, first locally (feature-adjacent cells), only then globally. Effort: days, not months; ~30-80 lines plus warp-composition and re-measure.

keyInsight: The current quadtree+registry mesher is not a homegrown oddity to be replaced â€” it IS the literature's answer (restricted-quadtree crack avoidance + edge-local shared-vertex functions + parametric-domain conforming CDT), so every rip-and-replace candidate either forfeits a hard constraint the rebuild exists to guarantee (DC and remeshers put vertices off the analytic surface and need forbidden repair passes; subdivision swaps the true surface for a limit surface) or re-converges to what already exists (GPU tessellation's crack rule = the registry). The diagnosed defect class â€” diagonal/helical crest slivers â€” is a METRIC problem, not a topology problem, and the codebase already contains the verified-dead metric-aware triangulation path (leaf.efg never populated; earClipMaxMinAngle never fires): activate it for days of effort before considering the only principled alternative, BLSURF-style curve-first Riemannian meshing, which solves the same root cause for months of robustness engineering and should remain the documented fallback triggered only by a measured ceiling of the in-place fix.
